metadata.scope: Residential lease law, with Hoboken-specific additions
metadata.source: NJ Revised Statutes (public domain), Hoboken Municipal Code, federal lead paint disclosure rules
metadata.disclaimer: Curated subset for hackathon use. Not exhaustive. Not legal advice.

statutes.title: Security deposit cap and interest-bearing account requirement
statutes.full_text: Whenever money or other form of security shall be deposited or advanced on a contract, lease or license agreement for the use or rental of real property as security for performance of the contract or agreement or to be applied to payments upon such contract or agreement when due, such money or other form of security, until repaid or so applied including the tenant's portion of the interest or earnings accumulated thereon as hereinafter provided, shall continue to be the property of the person making such deposit or advance and shall be held in trust by the person with whom such deposit or advance shall be made for the use in accordance with the terms of the contract, lease or agreement and shall not be mingled with the personal property or become an asset of the person receiving the same. The person receiving money so deposited or advanced shall: a. Invest that money in shares of any savings and loan association or savings bank or in any bank or banking institution insured by an agency of the federal government in this State, in a money market fund... or deposit the money in a money market account... b. The person investing the security deposit shall notify in writing each of the persons making such security deposit or advance, giving the name and address of the investment company, State or federally chartered bank, savings bank or savings and loan association in which the deposit or investment of security money is made, the type of account in which the security deposit is deposited or invested, the current rate of interest for that account, and the amount of such deposit or investment...
statutes.plain_summary: A landlord cannot demand more than 1.5 months' rent as a security deposit. The deposit remains the tenant's property — the landlord just holds it in trust. The landlord must place it in a separate interest-bearing account (savings bank, money market, or insured account) and tell the tenant in writing where it is and the interest rate. The tenant is entitled to the interest earned, paid out annually or credited toward rent.
common_violations: Lease demands 2 or 3 months' rent as security deposit (illegal — capped at 1.5 months)
Lease silent on which institution holds the deposit or what interest rate applies
Landlord commingles deposit with personal funds (illegal)
Lease states deposit is non-refundable (illegal — deposit is tenant's property)
Lease tries to convert security deposit into 'last month's rent' without consent

statutes.title: Annual interest payment on security deposit
statutes.full_text: The person receiving the security deposit shall pay the tenant the interest or earnings accrued on the security deposit annually as either a payment in cash or as a credit toward the payment of rent due. The interest or earnings shall be paid at the end of each year of the tenancy.
statutes.plain_summary: The landlord must pay the tenant any interest the deposit has earned every year — either as cash or as a credit on next month's rent. This is automatic; the tenant does not have to ask.
common_violations: Landlord keeps the interest on the security deposit (illegal — interest belongs to tenant)
Lease silent on interest payment, and landlord never sends one
Lease tries to assign deposit interest to landlord as a fee

statutes.title: Return of security deposit within 30 days
statutes.full_text: Within 30 days after the termination of the tenant's lease or licensee's agreement, the owner or lessee shall return by personal delivery, registered or certified mail the sum so deposited plus the tenant's portion of the interest or earnings accumulated thereon, less any charges expended in accordance with the terms of a contract, lease, or agreement, to the tenant or licensee, or, in the case of a rent security deposit, to the tenant. Any deductions made from the deposit shall be itemized and the tenant or licensee notified thereof by registered or certified mail together with the amount of the balance...
statutes.plain_summary: When the lease ends, the landlord has 30 days to return the security deposit (plus interest), minus any deductions. Deductions must be itemized in writing and sent by registered or certified mail. No itemization, no lawful deduction.
common_violations: Lease gives landlord more than 30 days to return deposit (illegal)
Lease lets landlord deduct without itemizing (illegal — itemization is mandatory)
Landlord deducts vague 'cleaning fees' or 'wear and tear' that aren't damages (unenforceable)
Lease requires tenant to forfeit deposit if leaving early without specific damages (often unenforceable)

statutes.title: Double damages for wrongful retention of security deposit
statutes.full_text: In any action by a tenant or licensee for the return of moneys due under this section, the court upon finding for the tenant or licensee shall award recovery of double the amount of said moneys, together with full costs of any action and, in the court's discretion, reasonable attorney's fees.
statutes.plain_summary: If the tenant has to sue to get the deposit back and wins, the court awards double the wrongfully withheld amount, plus court costs, plus possibly attorney's fees. This penalty exists to deter landlords from gambling on tenants not suing.
common_violations: Lease tries to waive the tenant's right to double damages (unenforceable)
Lease forces tenant to sue in a venue that complicates recovery (potentially unconscionable)

statutes.title: Anti-Eviction Act — just-cause requirement for residential eviction
statutes.full_text: No lessee or tenant or the assigns, under-tenants or legal representatives of such lessee or tenant may be removed by the Superior Court from any house, building, mobile home or land in a mobile home park or tenement leased for residential purposes, other than (1) owner-occupied premises with not more than two rental units or a hotel, motel or other guesthouse or part thereof rented to a transient guest or seasonal tenant... except upon establishment of one of the following grounds as good cause: a. The person fails to pay rent due and owing under the lease... b. The person has continued to be, after written notice to cease, so disorderly... c. The person has willfully or by reason of gross negligence caused or allowed destruction, damage or injury to the premises...
statutes.plain_summary: In New Jersey, a residential landlord cannot evict a tenant just because the lease is up. They must establish one of the specific grounds listed in this statute — non-payment, disorderly conduct, property damage, lease violation after written notice, etc. This is one of the strongest tenant-protection laws in the country. It applies even after the lease term ends, unless the building is owner-occupied with two or fewer units.
common_violations: Lease says landlord can terminate at end of term for any reason (largely unenforceable for non-exempt buildings)
Lease says tenant 'must vacate' at end of lease without renewal (unenforceable absent just cause)
Lease tries to waive Anti-Eviction Act protections (unenforceable — protections are non-waivable)
Lease threatens self-help eviction (illegal — only Superior Court can order eviction)

statutes.title: Retaliatory eviction prohibition
statutes.full_text: No landlord of premises or units to which this act is applicable shall serve a notice to quit upon any tenant or institute any action against a tenant to recover possession of premises, whether by summary dispossess proceedings, civil action for the possession of land, or otherwise: a. As a reprisal for the tenant's efforts to secure or enforce any rights under the laws of the State of New Jersey, or its governmental subdivisions, or of the United States; or b. As a reprisal for the tenant's good faith complaint to a governmental authority of the landlord's alleged violation of any health or safety law, regulation, code or ordinance, or State law or regulation which has as its objective the regulation of premises used for dwelling purposes; or c. As a reprisal for the tenant's being an organizer of, a member of, or involved in any activities of, any lawful organization...
statutes.plain_summary: A landlord cannot evict (or threaten to evict) a tenant in retaliation for the tenant exercising legal rights, complaining to a government agency about housing conditions, or organizing with other tenants. If eviction follows soon after such tenant action, NJ courts presume retaliation.
common_violations: Lease lets landlord terminate after tenant complains to housing authority (unenforceable)
Lease prohibits tenant from contacting government inspectors (unenforceable)
Lease prohibits tenant organization or unionization (unenforceable)

statutes.title: Truth in Renting Act — mandatory tenant rights summary
statutes.full_text: The Department of Community Affairs shall prepare a written statement, in both English and Spanish, of the established legal rights and responsibilities of tenants and landlords of rental dwelling units, except owner-occupied premises of not more than two rental units. The department shall distribute the statement to all rental property owners by such means as the department in its discretion deems appropriate, and the rental property owners shall distribute the statement to their tenants...
statutes.plain_summary: The NJ Department of Community Affairs publishes an official tenant rights statement that landlords are required to distribute to tenants in non-exempt buildings. Failure to provide it is itself a violation. The statement covers core tenant rights and is meant to ensure tenants are not signing in ignorance.
common_violations: Lease silent on Truth in Renting requirement; landlord never delivered the statement
Lease tries to waive Truth in Renting Act protections (unenforceable)

statutes.title: Implied warranty of habitability
statutes.full_text: Every residential lease in New Jersey contains an implied warranty of habitability — the landlord's promise that the dwelling is fit for habitation. This warranty cannot be waived. The landlord must maintain the premises in compliance with applicable housing codes, including providing essential services such as heat, hot water, plumbing, and structural safety. See also Marini v. Ireland, 56 N.J. 130 (1970), establishing tenant's right to repair-and-deduct, and Berzito v. Gambino, 63 N.J. 460 (1973), establishing tenant's right to withhold rent in proportion to defective conditions.
statutes.plain_summary: Every NJ residential lease automatically includes a guarantee that the apartment is livable — heat, hot water, working plumbing, no structural hazards, no major code violations. The landlord cannot make the tenant waive this. If the landlord fails to maintain habitable conditions, the tenant has multiple remedies recognized by NJ courts: repair-and-deduct, rent withholding in proportion to the defect, or constructive eviction (treating the lease as terminated).
common_violations: Lease says tenant accepts unit 'as-is' with no landlord obligation to repair (unenforceable as to habitability)
Lease tries to waive implied warranty of habitability (unenforceable — non-waivable)
Lease shifts all repair responsibility to tenant including structural and habitability (unenforceable)
Lease prohibits tenant repair-and-deduct (unenforceable for habitability defects)

statutes.title: Five-day grace period and late fee limits for senior/disabled tenants; common law for others
statutes.full_text: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, no recipient of Social Security Old Age, Survivors and Disability Insurance, Supplemental Security Income, Old Age Assistance or Disability Assistance shall be required to pay a late fee for the late payment of rent unless and until five business days have passed after the date by which the rent is due...
statutes.plain_summary: By statute, senior citizens and disabled tenants on Social Security or similar benefits get a mandatory 5-business-day grace period before any late fee applies. For other tenants, NJ has no statutory grace period, but late fees must still be reasonable and not punitive — courts have struck down late fees that function as penalties rather than reasonable estimates of landlord harm.
common_violations: Lease imposes late fee on day rent is due, even for senior/disabled tenants (illegal for protected class)
Late fee is a flat $100+ regardless of late amount (potentially punitive and unenforceable)
Late fee compounds daily without cap (often struck down as punitive)
Lease doesn't disclose grace period for protected tenants (Truth in Renting violation)

statutes.title: Application fee and tenant screening cost limits
statutes.full_text: A landlord may not charge a prospective tenant an application fee, or any other fee in connection with the application process, that exceeds the actual cost of obtaining a credit report, criminal background check, and any other actual third-party screening costs documented to the prospective tenant. A landlord shall provide an itemized receipt of any such fees upon request.
statutes.plain_summary: An application fee can only cover the landlord's actual third-party screening costs (credit check, background check, etc.). It cannot be a flat 'admin fee' or a profit center. The landlord must provide itemized documentation if asked.
common_violations: Lease/application charges $100+ fee without itemized cost basis (likely unenforceable)
Lease bundles 'application fee' with other charges (must be separately documented)
Application fee non-refundable even if landlord doesn't run actual screening (potentially fraud)

statutes.title: Federal lead-based paint disclosure (Title X)
statutes.full_text: Lessors of target housing (residential dwellings built before 1978) shall, before the lessee is obligated under any lease: (1) provide the lessee with a lead hazard information pamphlet... (2) disclose to the lessee the presence of any known lead-based paint or lead-based paint hazards in the housing... and provide to the lessee any records or reports concerning lead-based paint or lead-based paint hazards available to the lessor... (3) include in the lease... an attachment which shall contain a Lead Warning Statement and a statement signed by the lessee affirming receipt of the information.
statutes.plain_summary: If the building was built before 1978 (which is most of Hoboken's housing stock), federal law requires the landlord to (a) give the tenant the EPA's lead hazard pamphlet, (b) disclose any known lead paint or hazards, (c) attach a signed Lead Warning Statement to the lease. Failure can void portions of the lease and exposes the landlord to civil penalties up to $11,000 per violation.
common_violations: Pre-1978 building lease silent on lead paint disclosure (federal violation)
No EPA pamphlet provided
No Lead Warning Statement attached or signed
Landlord knows of lead hazards and doesn't disclose (federal violation)

statutes.title: Landlord registration statement requirement
statutes.full_text: Every owner of a multiple dwelling, as defined in P.L. 1967, c. 76 (C. 55:13A-3), or of a dwelling consisting of more than two units... shall, within 30 days after creating the relationship of landlord and tenant... file a certificate of registration of the rental premises with the Bureau of Housing Inspection...
statutes.plain_summary: Owners of buildings with 3+ rental units must register the property with the NJ Bureau of Housing Inspection within 30 days of any new tenancy. The certificate includes the owner's identity and contact information for service of legal process. A landlord who fails to register cannot bring an eviction action against the tenant until they comply.
common_violations: Lease in 3+ unit building silent on registration (often a sign landlord hasn't registered)
Landlord uses LLC shell without disclosing actual owner identity for service
Eviction filed by unregistered landlord (defense to eviction)

statutes.citation: Hoboken Municipal Code Section 155 (Rent Control Ordinance)
statutes.title: Hoboken rent control — applies to most pre-1987 multi-unit buildings
statutes.full_text: Hoboken's Rent Control Ordinance limits annual rent increases on covered units to a percentage tied to the CPI, with hardship and capital improvement exceptions. Generally covers buildings constructed before 1987 with four or more units. Tenants in covered units have the right to a Rent Control Board hearing on disputed increases. Vacancy decontrol is permitted only in narrow circumstances, and most renewals must comply with the cap.
statutes.plain_summary: Hoboken has rent control. Most older multi-family buildings (built before 1987 with 4+ units) are subject to it. Annual rent increases are capped at a CPI-linked percentage published by the Hoboken Rent Control Board, typically 1-4%. A landlord raising rent above the cap is likely violating the ordinance, and the tenant can challenge it. Many Stevens students sign leases in covered buildings and never know they're rent-controlled.
common_violations: Lease imposes 8-15% annual increase on a rent-controlled unit (almost certainly illegal)
Lease silent on rent control status for an obviously covered building
Landlord uses 'capital improvement surcharge' to disguise above-cap increase (must be Board-approved)
Vacancy decontrol asserted without basis

statutes.citation: Common law: Marini v. Ireland, 56 N.J. 130 (1970)
statutes.title: Repair-and-deduct remedy for habitability defects
statutes.full_text: Where the landlord fails to make necessary repairs that affect habitability after notice, the tenant may make the repairs and deduct the cost from rent. The remedy applies to defects that materially affect the tenant's ability to use the premises for residential purposes. Marini established this as a common-law right that cannot be contracted away.
statutes.plain_summary: If the landlord fails to make a habitability-related repair after being notified, the tenant can pay for the repair themselves and deduct it from the next month's rent. This is a NJ Supreme Court doctrine, not a statute, but it has the same force. A lease cannot waive it.
common_violations: Lease prohibits tenant from making any repairs or deducting from rent (unenforceable for habitability defects)
Lease requires landlord pre-approval for any tenant repair (unenforceable when landlord is non-responsive)
